brief.name: RE-1 — boutique off-market acquisitions team (LA)  // JOHNNY-EDITABLE name/handle
brief.what_they_do: Founder-led team (2-5) doing off-market / distressed acquisitions in predetermined key areas of LA. Just completed a major project cycle and closed 14 properties in the last 12 months. Researches owners manually (title runs, BeenVerified-style lookups, driving-for-dollars). Holds a raw ~10k-row county data CSV. No CRM or automation tooling. Wants to expand to other cities.

signals.detail: we finally sold everything on it. So right now I have like no houses
The way that we've typically found distressed properties is straight up just driving
Our email thing has been like consistently 30% wrong
fifteen thousand dollars and it's like 40 enriched... this is just way too much so... we'll stick doing it manually
either we need to hire someone else on the team or i could get... like an assistant... the goal is is to start moving to other cities

i'm johnny — an independent AI-automations marketer. i build the data+story layer for sales teams: scraping, enrichment, CRM-from-scratch, agent systems. receipts: built Vortex for a marketing agency on a signed $5,040 two-month contract; my LinkedIn outreach pulled ~40% reply rates vs 2% cold email. the system replaces the manual research hire.
PERSON SCRAPE DEGRADED: person stage skipped: no person inputs on the run (linkedinUrl/xHandle/name all absent)
Real-estate investor client agreed $4-5k flat + 10-20% commission ('anywhere from 4 5 K would be sick')
Marketing agency signed fixed $5,040 / 2 months / 4 modules + ~$250/mo running costs
'name + linkedin' Google-search hack: ~85% hit rate where scrapers get accounts flagged
LinkedIn outreach ~40% reply rate vs cold email ~2% ('if you're doing like good')
The pricing anchor to beat: refused vendor at '$15,000 and it's like 40 enriched'

generations.story:
THE GOAL:
Close the next 14 properties — or more — but this time without rebuilding the pipeline from scratch by hand. Carlos's own words: 'the goal is to start moving to other cities.' The 10k-row county CSV sitting on his desktop is the seed; the ambition is a repeatable acquisition engine that works in LA and scales to the next market without adding a full-time hire.

THE OBSTACLE:
The pipeline is empty right now. [pipeline_gap] 'We finally sold everything on it. So right now I have like no houses.' Every deal cycle ends the same way: the data gets worked, the properties get closed, and then the team is back at zero — driving neighborhoods, running title manually, cross-checking BeenVerified lookups one row at a time. [manual_research] That grind produced 14 closes in 12 months, which is real, but it doesn't compound. And the contact data feeding that outreach is [data_pain] 'consistently 30% wrong' on email — meaning roughly 3,000 rows of that 10k CSV are quietly burning effort and credibility on every send.

THE OLD WAY FAILS:
The manual stack had a ceiling and Carlos already hit it. To go to a second city, he estimated he'd need [expansion_intent] 'to hire someone else on the team or... like an assistant.' That's a recurring payroll line to replicate a research workflow that is already broken — 30%-wrong data, no CRM, no enrichment layer, no way to sort which of the 10k rows are actually forced-sale signals worth calling today. When he priced out fixing it, the vendor quote came back at [vendor_refusal] '$15,000 and it's like 40 enriched... this is just way too much so we'll stick doing it manually.' Sticking manually means the next city expansion is a copy-paste of the same bottleneck, not a system.

THE BETTER PATH:
Johnny builds the data-and-story layer Carlos is missing — scraping, enrichment, forced-sale signal sorting, and a durable CRM structure — on that existing 10k-row CSV, without the $15k vendor price tag. [seller_pack] A comparable real-estate investor engagement landed at a $4–5k flat plus 10–20% commission structure. [seller_pack] The 'name + LinkedIn' Google-search method hits ~85% contact accuracy where scraper tools get accounts flagged — directly attacking the 30%-wrong email problem at the source. The output isn't a one-time file drop; it's a rerunnable system that travels to the next city without rebuilding from scratch. [seller_pack] The marketing agency parallel: $5,040 / two months / four modules, ~$250/mo to keep running — a system that replaces the manual research hire, not a vendor invoice for 40 enriched rows.
Opening line, in Johnny's voice: 'You've got 10,000 rows and a proven close rate — the only thing missing is a system that tells you which 200 to call first, so the next city doesn't start from zero the way LA just did.'

THE BETTER OUTCOME:
Carlos's 10k CSV becomes a living, sortable pipeline — forced-sale signals surfaced, contact accuracy pushed toward 85%, and a CRM structure that doesn't evaporate when the current cycle closes. [expansion_intent] The second city gets stood up on the same system, not a new hire. The next 14 closes start from enriched, prioritized data instead of a blank spreadsheet and a windshield. He's in a powerful position because the raw material — the county data, the 12-month track record, the acquisition criteria — already exists; the only move left is to stop rebuilding the engine by hand every time.

10k-row county CSV sitting on his desktop
repeatable acquisition engine that works in LA and scales to the next market
3,000 rows of that 10k CSV are quietly burning effort
produced 14 closes in 12 months

rerunnable system that travels to the next city without rebuilding from scratch
pushed toward 85%

generations.story:
THE GOAL:
Close the next wave of off-market distressed acquisitions in LA — and then replicate that playbook in new cities. In Carlos's own words from the discovery call: "the goal is to start moving to other cities" [expansion_intent].

THE OBSTACLE:
The pipeline is empty right now. Carlos said it plainly: "we finally sold everything on it. So right now I have like no houses" [pipeline_gap]. The 10k-row county CSV they're sitting on is raw and unreliable — their own email data has been "consistently 30% wrong" [data_pain]. A broken contact list means outreach stalls before it starts, and a stalled pipeline in a new city is a dead expansion.

THE OLD WAY FAILS:
The team finds distressed properties by "straight up just driving" [manual_research] — a method that doesn't scale past one market. When they looked at tooling to fix the data problem, the vendor quoted "fifteen thousand dollars and it's like 40 enriched" — so they walked away and said "we'll stick doing it manually" [vendor_refusal]. Manual driving can't cover LA thoroughly AND scout Phoenix or Vegas simultaneously. The team is already weighing whether to "hire someone else" or bring on an assistant [expansion_intent] — a recurring payroll cost that still doesn't solve the 30%-wrong data sitting in the CSV.

THE BETTER PATH:
Johnny builds the data-and-story layer that replaces that manual research hire. He takes the raw county CSV, enriches it to verified contacts using a name + LinkedIn Google-search method that hits ~85% accuracy where scrapers get flagged [seller_pack], and structures it into a durable CRM system that's rerunnable — not automation slop that needs to be rebuilt every two months [seller_positioning]. A comparable real-estate investor engagement landed at a $4-5k flat fee plus 10-20% commission [seller_pack], well under the $15k vendor Carlos already refused [vendor_refusal]. The enriched list then feeds LinkedIn outreach that pulls ~40% reply rates versus the ~2% cold email is delivering [seller_pack].
Opening angle (Johnny's voice): "You've got a 10k-row county file and a 30%-wrong email list — before you hire anyone or buy another city's data, let me show you what that file actually looks like when it's clean."

THE BETTER OUTCOME:
Carlos's team enters the next acquisition cycle with a verified, story-enriched lead list pulled from the CSV they already own — no new vendor dependency, no recurring headcount. When the LA pipeline closes again, the same system reruns on a new county file for the next city. Expansion becomes a data operation, not a hiring decision. Carlos is in a powerful position: he already has the raw material; the only thing missing is the enrichment layer that makes it actionable.

10k-row county file

generations.story:
THE GOAL:
Close the next cycle of off-market acquisitions — and not just in LA. The team just cleared their entire portfolio ('we finally sold everything on it') and the founder's stated ambition is city expansion: 'the goal is to start moving to other cities' [expansion_intent].

THE OBSTACLE:
The pipeline is empty right now. His own words: 'right now I have like no houses' [pipeline_gap]. The machine that filled it before — driving for dollars, manual title runs, BeenVerified-style lookups — is slow, person-dependent, and produces contact data that is 'consistently 30% wrong' [data_pain]. A 30% bad-data rate means roughly one in three outreach attempts is dead on arrival before a single conversation starts.

THE OLD WAY FAILS:
He already tried to buy his way out of the manual grind. A vendor quoted $15,000 for roughly 40 enriched records — he walked: 'this is just way too much so... we'll stick doing it manually' [vendor_refusal]. The fallback is hiring: 'either we need to hire someone else on the team or I could get... like an assistant' [expansion_intent]. But a hire doesn't solve the 30%-wrong data problem [data_pain] — it just adds payroll to a broken research loop. And neither a hire nor a $15k vendor scales to a second or third city without rebuilding from scratch each time.

THE BETTER PATH:
Johnny builds the data-and-story layer that replaces that hire — scraping, enrichment, CRM-from-scratch, rerunnable agent systems that don't need to be rebuilt every two months [seller_positioning]. The 'name + LinkedIn' Google-search method hits ~85% where scrapers get accounts flagged [seller_pack], directly attacking the 30%-wrong contact problem [data_pain]. A comparable real-estate investor engagement landed at a $4–5k flat plus 10–20% commission structure [seller_pack] — a fraction of the $15,000 walk-away price [vendor_refusal], and the output is a durable system that reruns on the next city without starting over.
Opening angle (Johnny's voice): 'You're sitting at the exact moment between cycles — pipeline zero, expansion on the table — and the question is whether the next city runs on the same manual loop or on something you can actually scale.'

THE BETTER OUTCOME:
The team enters the next acquisition cycle with enriched, contact-verified leads pulled from their existing county data — not 30%-wrong emails but validated owner contacts — and a rerunnable system they own. When the LA pipeline refills and the first new city comes online, the research layer is already there: same system, new geography, no rebuild, no new hire required. The founder gets to spend the expansion budget on deals, not on data vendors charging $15k for 40 rows.

The team just cleared their entire portfolio
The machine that filled it before — driving for dollars, manual title runs, BeenVerified-style lookups — is slow, person-dependent
A 30% bad-data rate means roughly one in three outreach attempts is dead on arrival before a single conversation starts
A comparable real-estate investor engagement landed at a $4–5k flat plus 10–20% commission structure

And neither a hire nor a $15k vendor scales to a second or third city without rebuilding from scratch each time.

THE BETTER PATH:
Johnny builds the data-and-story layer that replaces that hire — scraping, enrichment, CRM-from-scratch, rerunnable agent systems that don't need to be rebuilt every two months [seller_positioning]. The 'name + LinkedIn' Google-search method hits ~85% where scrapers get accounts flagged [seller_pack], directly attacking the 30%-wrong contact problem [data_pain]. A comparable real-estate investor engagement landed at a $4–5k flat plus 10–20% commission structure [seller_pack] — a fraction of the $15,000 walk-away price [vendor_refusal], and the output is a durable system that reruns on the next city without starting over.
Opening angle (Johnny's voice): 'You're sitting at the exact moment between cycles — pipeline zero, expansion on the table — and the question is whether the next city runs on the same manual loop or on something you can actually scale.'

THE BETTER OUTCOME:
The team enters the next acquisition cycle with enriched, contact-verified leads pulled from their existing county data — not 30%-wrong emails but validated owner contacts — and a rerunnable system they own. When the LA pipeline refills and the first new city comes online, the research layer is already there: same system, new geography, no rebuild, no new hire required. The founder gets to spend the expansion budget on deals, not on data vendors charging $15k for 40 rows.

event.story:
THE GOAL:
Close the next 14 properties — or more — but this time without rebuilding the pipeline from scratch by hand. Carlos's own words: 'the goal is to start moving to other cities.' The 10k-row county CSV sitting on his desktop is the seed; the ambition is a repeatable acquisition engine that works in LA and scales to the next market without adding a full-time hire.

THE OBSTACLE:
The pipeline is empty right now. [pipeline_gap] 'We finally sold everything on it. So right now I have like no houses.' Every deal cycle ends the same way: the data gets worked, the properties get closed, and then the team is back at zero — driving neighborhoods, running title manually, cross-checking BeenVerified lookups one row at a time. [manual_research] That grind produced 14 closes in 12 months, which is real, but it doesn't compound. And the contact data feeding that outreach is [data_pain] 'consistently 30% wrong' on email — meaning roughly 3,000 rows of that 10k CSV are quietly burning effort and credibility on every send.

THE OLD WAY FAILS:
The manual stack had a ceiling and Carlos already hit it. To go to a second city, he estimated he'd need [expansion_intent] 'to hire someone else on the team or... like an assistant.' That's a recurring payroll line to replicate a research workflow that is already broken — 30%-wrong data, no CRM, no enrichment layer, no way to sort which of the 10k rows are actually forced-sale signals worth calling today. When he priced out fixing it, the vendor quote came back at [vendor_refusal] '$15,000 and it's like 40 enriched... this is just way too much so we'll stick doing it manually.' Sticking manually means the next city expansion is a copy-paste of the same bottleneck, not a system.

THE BETTER PATH:
Johnny builds the data-and-story layer Carlos is missing — scraping, enrichment, forced-sale signal sorting, and a durable CRM structure — on that existing 10k-row CSV, without the $15k vendor price tag. [seller_pack] A comparable real-estate investor engagement landed at a $4–5k flat plus 10–20% commission structure. [seller_pack] The 'name + LinkedIn' Google-search method hits ~85% contact accuracy where scraper tools get accounts flagged — directly attacking the 30%-wrong email problem at the source. The output isn't a one-time file drop; it's a rerunnable system that travels to the next city without rebuilding from scratch. [seller_pack] The marketing agency parallel: $5,040 / two months / four modules, ~$250/mo to keep running — a system that replaces the manual research hire, not a vendor invoice for 40 enriched rows.
Opening line, in Johnny's voice: 'You've got 10,000 rows and a proven close rate — the only thing missing is a system that tells you which 200 to call first, so the next city doesn't start from zero the way LA just did.'

THE BETTER OUTCOME:
Carlos's 10k CSV becomes a living, sortable pipeline — forced-sale signals surfaced, contact accuracy pushed toward 85%, and a CRM structure that doesn't evaporate when the current cycle closes. [expansion_intent] The second city gets stood up on the same system, not a new hire. The next 14 closes start from enriched, prioritized data instead of a blank spreadsheet and a windshield. He's in a powerful position because the raw material — the county data, the 12-month track record, the acquisition criteria — already exists; the only move left is to stop rebuilding the engine by hand every time.

event.story:
THE GOAL:
Close the next wave of off-market distressed acquisitions in LA — and then replicate that playbook in new cities. In Carlos's own words from the discovery call: "the goal is to start moving to other cities" [expansion_intent].

THE OBSTACLE:
The pipeline is empty right now. Carlos said it plainly: "we finally sold everything on it. So right now I have like no houses" [pipeline_gap]. The 10k-row county CSV they're sitting on is raw and unreliable — their own email data has been "consistently 30% wrong" [data_pain]. A broken contact list means outreach stalls before it starts, and a stalled pipeline in a new city is a dead expansion.

THE OLD WAY FAILS:
The team finds distressed properties by "straight up just driving" [manual_research] — a method that doesn't scale past one market. When they looked at tooling to fix the data problem, the vendor quoted "fifteen thousand dollars and it's like 40 enriched" — so they walked away and said "we'll stick doing it manually" [vendor_refusal]. Manual driving can't cover LA thoroughly AND scout Phoenix or Vegas simultaneously. The team is already weighing whether to "hire someone else" or bring on an assistant [expansion_intent] — a recurring payroll cost that still doesn't solve the 30%-wrong data sitting in the CSV.

THE BETTER PATH:
Johnny builds the data-and-story layer that replaces that manual research hire. He takes the raw county CSV, enriches it to verified contacts using a name + LinkedIn Google-search method that hits ~85% accuracy where scrapers get flagged [seller_pack], and structures it into a durable CRM system that's rerunnable — not automation slop that needs to be rebuilt every two months [seller_positioning]. A comparable real-estate investor engagement landed at a $4-5k flat fee plus 10-20% commission [seller_pack], well under the $15k vendor Carlos already refused [vendor_refusal]. The enriched list then feeds LinkedIn outreach that pulls ~40% reply rates versus the ~2% cold email is delivering [seller_pack].
Opening angle (Johnny's voice): "You've got a 10k-row county file and a 30%-wrong email list — before you hire anyone or buy another city's data, let me show you what that file actually looks like when it's clean."

THE BETTER OUTCOME:
Carlos's team enters the next acquisition cycle with a verified, story-enriched lead list pulled from the CSV they already own — no new vendor dependency, no recurring headcount. When the LA pipeline closes again, the same system reruns on a new county file for the next city. Expansion becomes a data operation, not a hiring decision. Carlos is in a powerful position: he already has the raw material; the only thing missing is the enrichment layer that makes it actionable.
